summary: Postinstall dropper fetches and executes encrypted Windows PE payload, then self-deletes
details: analysis-chart@2.0.12 advertises itself as a chart-analysis library but its `postinstall` hook (`install-hook.js`, declared in package.json) is a Windows in-process PE loader. On `npm install`, install-hook.js downloads an opaque binary from `https://github.com/Dimitrijenco/Sticky_note/releases/download/v6/payload.bin` (a personal GitHub repo unrelated to the stated package purpose or declared author), XOR-decrypts it with key 0x42, validates the MZ header, and uses koffi to call kernel32 `VirtualAlloc` / `VirtualProtect` / `RtlMoveMemory` / `CreateThread` to map and execute the decrypted PE inside the Node.js process â€” giving the publisher arbitrary native-code execution on every installer's machine. After launching the payload, the script writes and detaches a `cleanup.js` that, after a 3-second delay, recursively removes the package directory from `node_modules`, strips the `analysis-chart` entry from the user's project `package.json` across all dependency sections, and unlinks `install-hook.js` and `cleanup.js` â€” anti-forensic cleanup so the running payload remains while traceable package presence is removed. The `index.js` chart-analysis API and the `Elena Vogt <elena@analysis-chart.io>` author metadata are cover; the payload host (`Dimitrijenco/Sticky_note`) has no relation to the declared identity.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
